gold.rationale: The article inflates a mix of localized tornado damage, one rare tornado-emergency warning in Indiana, generic preparedness messaging, and Hawaii flood/severe-weather monitoring into a broader national tornado-emergency narrative. That false escalation is reinforced by edited and synthetic visuals that depict tornadoes and simultaneous tornado-emergency alerts that were not actually documented.

atoms: A large tornado funnel cloud is visible in the distance under dark storm clouds.
A white house in the foreground has significant roof damage with exposed wooden beams.
A large tree in the foreground is snapped in half near the trunk.
A red barn in the background has a collapsed roof.
A silo stands next to the damaged red barn.
Emergency vehicles, including a fire truck and police car, are parked on the left.
Debris is scattered across the grassy fields and road.
An American flag is flying on a pole near the white houses in the background.
A smartphone screenshot of the 'NEXRAD EMERGENCY' weather app.
A red banner at the top reads '4 Active Tornado Emergencies'.
A map of the United States shows red warning polygons over Missouri, Illinois, and New Jersey.
A smaller inset map shows a 'Tornado Emergency' alert over Hawaii.
Text labels identify 'Tornado Emergency St. Louis, MO', 'Southern Illinois', and 'Central New Jersey'.
Current conditions panel shows 72°F with rain and lightning icons.
The 'Alerts' panel lists 4 Tornado Emergencies, 11 Severe Thunderstorm Warnings, and 3 Flash Flood Warnings.
The time displayed is 9:41 and the date is May 25, 2024.
A screenshot of the 'DisasterAWARE Pro' software interface.
A map of the Hawaiian islands is displayed with blue and green shaded areas.
A legend on the right indicates 'Cumulative Rainfall Forecast (3 Day)' with color-coded inch ranges.
The map shows the Big Island of Hawaii with significant green and yellow shading indicating rainfall.
The top timeline bar shows dates in March 2026.
A sidebar on the left lists layers such as 'Demography', 'Infrastructure', and 'Hawaii'.
An infographic titled 'TORNADO TERMINOLOGY' with a 'MARCH 11 ACTIVE ALERT STATUS' banner.
Three columns are labeled 'WATCH' (yellow), 'WARNING' (red), and 'EMERGENCY' (purple).
The 'WATCH' column shows a storm cloud and text 'CONDITIONS ARE FAVORABLE FOR TORNADOES TO FORM'.
The 'WARNING' column shows a radar screen and text 'TORNADO SPOTTED OR INDICATED BY RADAR'.
The 'EMERGENCY' column shows a tornado near a house and text 'SEVERE THREAT TO HUMAN LIFE AND CATASTROPHIC DAMAGE'.
A logo for 'JANDILS ATTORNEYS AT LAW, LC' and 'STORM TEAM' is visible in the top right.
A large metal building structure is collapsed and twisted.
A white semi-trailer is parked next to the wreckage.
A red pickup truck is parked on the right side of the frame.
The ground is wet with puddles, indicating recent rain.
The sky is overcast with grey clouds.
Debris from the building's roof and walls is scattered on the ground.
A screenshot of the 'DisasterAWARE Pro' software interface showing a map of Hawaii.
The map displays 'DFIRM Flood Zones' with red outlines around coastal areas.
A legend on the right lists flood hazard types like '0.2% annual chance (500yr) floodplain'.
A pop-up window on the map reads 'Flood - Hawaii Islands, United States'.
The sidebar on the left shows 'Hazard Zones' and 'DFIRM Flood...' are selected.
The top timeline bar shows dates in March 2026.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A rural landscape scene showing the aftermath of a tornado. A large funnel cloud is visible in the distance. In the foreground, a white house has a damaged roof, and a large tree is snapped in half. A red barn in the background is also damaged. Emergency vehicles are present on the left.
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image depicts tornado damage and a funnel cloud, consistent with the post's description of events in Illinois.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A screenshot of a weather app called 'NEXRAD EMERGENCY'. It displays a map of the US with red 'Tornado Emergency' alerts over Missouri, Illinois, and New Jersey. A separate alert is shown for Hawaii. The date on the screen is May 25, 2024.
visual_indicators.ocr_text: NEXRAD EMERGENCY | United States | 4 Active Tornado Emergencies | Multiple areas at extreme risk. Take immediate shelter. | 9:38 PM CDT May 25, 2024 | Tornado Emergency St. Louis, MO | Tornado Emergency Southern Illinois | Tornado Emergency Central New Jersey | Tornado Emergency Hawaii | CURRENT CONDITIONS 72°F | NEXT FEW HOURS | ALERTS | Tornado Emergency 4 | Severe Thunderstorm Warning 11 | Flash Flood Warning 3 | Special Weather Statement 2
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows a weather app with tornado emergencies, but the date (May 25, 2024) conflicts with the post's context of March 11.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A screenshot of the 'DisasterAWARE Pro' software. It shows a map of Hawaii with a 'Cumulative Rainfall Forecast (3 Day)' overlay. The Big Island shows significant rainfall predictions. The timeline at the top indicates March 2026.
visual_indicators.ocr_text: DisasterAWARE Pro | Layers | Demography | Infrastructure | Hydrography | Hawaii | Hazards | Observations and ... | Cumulative Rainfall Forecast (3 Day) | 0 - 0.5 inches | 0.5 - 1 inches | ... | > 30 inches | Positions and Segments
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows a disaster monitoring tool for Hawaii, consistent with the post's mention of the Pacific Disaster Center and Kona Low, though the date is 2026.

visual_indicators.scene_description: An infographic explaining 'TORNADO TERMINOLOGY'. It has three columns: 'WATCH' (yellow), 'WARNING' (red), and 'EMERGENCY' (purple). It includes definitions and images for each level. A banner at the top says 'MARCH 11 ACTIVE ALERT STATUS'.

visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image explains tornado terminology and references 'March 11', aligning with the post's date and theme.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A photo of severe structural damage to a large metal building, likely a barn or warehouse. The roof and walls are twisted and collapsed. A white trailer and a red pickup truck are parked nearby on wet ground.
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows structural damage consistent with the post's description of tornado damage in rural areas.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A screenshot of the 'DisasterAWARE Pro' software showing a map of Hawaii. It displays 'DFIRM Flood Zones' with red outlines. A pop-up indicates 'Flood - Hawaii Islands'. The timeline shows March 2026.

visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows flood hazard mapping for Hawaii, consistent with the post's discussion of the Kona Low and flood threats in the Pacific.

retrieved_evidence.text: Meteorologists and warning‑coordination specialists in the United States employ a tiered set of terms—watches, warnings and emergencies—to convey escalating tornado risk, with the emergency designation reserved for large, destructive vortices threatening densely populated areas. Background materials used in public‑education campaigns often depict this hierarchy in graphic form, emphasising that a tornado emergency reflects an ongoing, life‑threatening situation rather than a routine alert. During the 11 March 2026 event from Illinois and Missouri to New Jersey and Hawaii, weather‑service offices repeatedly stressed this terminology so that communities would recognize when conditions had crossed the threshold into the most dangerous category and seek shelter immediately.

Substantial damage from overnight storm in southeastern St Clair County. Damage in rural Marissa, Lenzburg and St Libory.

National Weather Service's Damage Assessment Team is surveying damage to determine if a tornado touched down.

St. Clair County Emergency Management Director Herb Simmons tells KMOX, with his years of experience, believes the damage looks like tornado destruction.

The Pacific Disaster Center’s DisasterAWARE platform is a global hazard‑monitoring system frequently used by emergency managers in Hawaii to follow tropical cyclones, heavy rainfall and other regional threats. In accounts related to the 11 March 2026 weather pattern, officials highlighted how tools like DisasterAWARE can be adapted to track severe storm impacts, including rare instances of tornado‑related concerns across the islands. By displaying layered data on wind fields, precipitation and alerts, the Hawaii‑focused map view allows local authorities to coordinate shelter guidance, resource staging and public information even when the most intense tornado emergencies are concentrated on the continental United States.

Modern smartphone weather applications and broadcast dashboards increasingly aggregate warning data from the National Weather Service and other agencies, presenting users with colour‑coded markers for threats such as tornadoes, severe thunderstorms and flash floods. During the 11 March 2026 severe‑weather episode, observers noted how multiple red, high‑end alerts appeared simultaneously across parts of Illinois, Missouri, New Jersey and even Hawaii as officials escalated messaging. According to emergency‑management staff, such interfaces help residents visualize where tornado emergencies and particularly dangerous situations are unfolding in real time, reinforcing calls to move quickly to interior rooms or community shelters.

⚠️ 🚨 Considering today's forecasted weather we could see severe storms in parts of #NJ. Download the free Red Cross Emergency App for weather alerts like tornado warnings, and other helpful features like preparedness checklists. #NJwx

Southeastern St. Clair County in southern Illinois has experienced multiple severe‑weather events over recent decades, with its mix of residential areas, small businesses and rural properties leaving structures exposed to high winds and flying debris. In recent descriptions tied to the 11 March 2026 outbreak, local authorities reported collapsed roofs, damaged walls and overturned vehicles after strong storms swept through overnight. First responders indicated that the rapid intensification of the threat, as tornado emergencies and severe‑thunderstorm warnings were issued, challenged both search‑and‑rescue operations and efforts to restore power and reopen key transportation routes across this part of the Midwest.

Rural communities in Illinois and neighbouring Midwest states have long been vulnerable to severe spring weather, with farmsteads, grain silos and outbuildings often standing directly in the path of fast‑moving storm cells. In recent reports on the 11 March 2026 outbreak, residents described large funnels advancing over open fields toward barns and homes, while sirens and emergency alerts signalled tornado‑emergency conditions. Local officials in Illinois and Missouri indicated that rapid shifts in wind and visibility left little time to secure livestock, agricultural equipment and family shelters before debris began to accumulate around damaged farm buildings.

Local authorities in Illinois reported pockets of structural damage on March 11, 2026, including in southeastern St. Clair County, as severe storms moved through parts of the state. According to emergency management officials, crews were dispatched to survey affected neighborhoods, where fallen trees, damaged roofs and downed power lines were observed in scattered locations. Specialists are examining the pattern of the destruction to determine whether any tornadoes briefly touched down or whether the impacts were caused by straight-line winds, with a more detailed assessment expected in the following days.

Across the United States, including states like Illinois, Missouri and New Jersey, public‑safety campaigns around tornado season increasingly stress preparedness measures such as identifying safe rooms, assembling emergency kits and understanding warning terminology. In the wake of the 11 March 2026 wave of tornado emergencies, emergency‑management agencies reiterated guidance on sheltering below ground level where possible, staying away from windows and monitoring multiple information channels. According to officials, communities that had conducted regular drills and invested in reinforced shelters were better able to cope with the rapid escalation from standard severe‑weather advisories to emergency‑level alerts.

An EF1 tornado struck the Moro, Illinois, area in Madison County on Wednesday, March 11, 2026. The storm caused significant damage to several homes and farms in the region. Witnesses described terrifying moments as the tornado passed, tearing off roofs, toppling trees, and damaging structures. Emergency crews are assessing the extent of the damage and providing assistance to affected residents. Fortunately, there were no immediate reports of serious injuries, but the community now faces the arduous task of rebuilding. The event serves as a reminder of nature's unpredictable force.

Emergency management agencies and forecasters are increasingly relying on a mix of technology and field reporting to track and communicate severe weather threats, according to officials interviewed in recent days. Local authorities say mobile weather applications, highway and rooftop live cameras, and high-resolution radar imagery are now routinely used to pinpoint storm cores and identify developing rotation in rural and urban areas alike. After major outbreaks, damage assessment teams travel back roads and farm communities to document impacts on homes, power lines and crops, information that feeds into updated risk maps and helps guide future preparedness efforts.

A meteorologist with a national forecast center said in a recent technical briefing that the phrase "tornado emergency" represents the highest level of urgency used within tornado warning products and is reserved for extremely rare, high-impact situations. According to the expert, such wording is typically considered only when a confirmed, dangerous tornado is moving into a densely populated area and poses a significant risk to life and property. Officials emphasized that most tornado warnings do not reach this elevated status, and they urged the public to treat all tornado-related alerts seriously, regardless of the specific terminology used.

The Pacific Disaster Center recently issued guidance to Hawaii residents regarding an approaching Kona Low system that could bring a combination of heavy rainfall, damaging winds and strong thunderstorms to the islands. According to the agency, the disturbance may lead to flash flooding in low-lying and urban areas, while higher elevations could experience gusty conditions and localized power interruptions. Officials also noted a smaller but non-negligible potential for hail or brief tornado activity embedded within the broader storm pattern, urging communities to monitor updated forecasts and follow instructions from local emergency managers.

Just in Case...

Never hurts to go over Severe Weather safety, especially since we are expecting some severe storms later today. The potential is there for a Tornado or two. 

A Watch means 'watch'... but a Warning means take action now. 

An Emergency (God Forbid) is exceptionally rare for our region, but it's the highest sense of urgency with a tornado warning. This means a large and potentially deadly tornado is confirmed to be on the ground. There were a couple of these issued during the March 2012 Outbreak in Eastern KY and Western WV. 

In regards to your safe spot - a basement is always best, but the lowest floor away from windows near the center of your home isn't a bad second option.

Hospital and healthcare systems along the broad corridor from the Midwest to the East Coast prepare for tornado emergencies by coordinating surge capacity, securing backup power and organising triage protocols. In the context of the March 11, 2026 severe‑weather episode, facilities in Illinois and New Jersey reportedly reviewed mass‑casualty plans as alerts intensified, anticipating potential injuries from collapsed buildings and airborne debris. Emergency‑room staff, paramedics and regional health authorities emphasized the importance of maintaining clear communication links with 911 centres and incident‑command posts so that casualties could be transported safely despite damaged roads and intermittent telecommunications.

A regional weather observer reported what was described as an EF1-strength tornado near the community of Moro on March 11, 2026, citing indications of rotational damage to homes and outbuildings. According to local officials, several properties sustained roof damage and broken windows, and vehicles were reportedly displaced by strong winds in a narrow corridor. Emergency responders said there were no immediate reports of serious injuries, and residents who experienced minor damage were being assisted with temporary repairs and insurance contacts while survey teams worked to confirm the classification of the event.

Transportation infrastructure, from interstate highways in the Midwest to commuter rail corridors in the Northeast, is particularly vulnerable when tornado emergencies unfold across multiple regions. During the storms reported on 11 March 2026, authorities in Illinois, Missouri and New Jersey temporarily closed segments of roads and suspended some rail services as heavy debris, downed power lines and reduced visibility made travel hazardous. Public‑works departments and utility crews were placed on standby to clear fallen trees and restore service, while travellers were urged to delay nonessential journeys until the most intense cells and associated warnings had passed.

On March 11, 2026, forecasters and emergency officials in the United States monitored what they described as a significant threat for severe weather across several regions, according to early outlooks from national and local weather services. Meteorologists highlighted the potential for intense thunderstorms capable of producing strong winds, large hail and isolated tornadoes, particularly during the late afternoon and evening hours. Authorities in affected states urged residents to stay informed through broadcast alerts and phone-based warning systems, stressing that the overall extent and severity of the event would only become clear as storms developed through the day.

School districts and universities in areas affected by severe weather, including communities in Illinois and Missouri, often serve as focal points for both risk and resilience during major tornado outbreaks. Accounts related to the 11 March 2026 events describe administrators activating shelter‑in‑place procedures, moving students away from gymnasiums and portable classrooms into interior hallways or reinforced basements as tornado emergencies were declared. After the storms, educational facilities also functioned as distribution centres for supplies and information, demonstrating how pre‑planned emergency protocols can protect large groups when warnings escalate with little lead time.

The American Red Cross chapter in New Jersey issued a public advisory this week encouraging residents to prepare for the possibility of severe storms forecast in the coming days. According to the organization, households were urged to assemble basic emergency kits, review family communication plans and identify safe interior rooms in case of high winds or tornado activity. The Red Cross also recommended that people download a weather-alert application to receive timely warnings on mobile devices, noting that such tools can provide critical minutes of lead time when storms intensify quickly.

As severe weather season advances across the United States, emergency planners and broadcast meteorologists have been discussing the prospect of days when powerful storms affect regions from the Plains to the East Coast in rapid succession. According to several officials, scenarios are occasionally modeled in which significant tornado risk overlaps with densely populated corridors and key transportation routes, prompting heightened coordination between local, state and federal agencies. While such coast-to-coast high-impact events remain uncommon, authorities say they use realistic case studies to refine response plans and public messaging aimed at reducing casualties and damage.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post accurately reports specific weather events (Illinois tornadoes, Hawaii Kona Low, NJ alerts) corroborated by evidence, but fabricates specific high-precision survey data (wind speeds, path dimensions) and misattributes the source of the 'Tornado Emergency' warning to Indiana instead of the evidence-supported context or general rarity.
text_analysis.t1_evidence: The post claims specific survey data for the Illinois tornadoes ('peak winds estimated near 110 mph', 'path at about 1.6 miles long', 'maximum width of 300 yards', 'peak winds around 94 mph') which are not present in the evidence (src_402_1, src_402_2, news_402_2, news_402_4, news_402_10) that only mentions 'EF-1', 'significant damage', or 'surveying to determine if a tornado touched down'.
t1_problematic_subclaims: path at about 1.6 miles long and a maximum width of 300 yards
text_analysis.t3_evidence: The post states 'A tornado emergency was issued in northern Indiana overnight... as a dangerous storm approached Knox', but the evidence (src_402_5, news_402_4, news_402_10) only confirms an EF-1 tornado in Moro, Illinois, and general severe weather; there is no evidence of a 'Tornado Emergency' in Indiana, and src_402_5 associates the term 'Emergency' with historical outbreaks in KY/WV or general rarity, not a specific current event in Indiana.
t3_problematic_subclaims: A tornado emergency was issued in northern Indiana overnight and into early March 11 as a dangerous storm approached Knox
text_analysis.global_assessment: The post is a well-written narrative that correctly identifies the broad weather events but inserts fabricated specific metrics and mislocates a specific high-level warning, making it partially reliable but factually flawed in details.

images.scene_description: A rural scene showing a tornado funnel in the distance and significant damage to a white farmhouse and red barn in the foreground, with debris scattered across the lawn and a large tree snapped in half.
images.v1_evidence: The image exhibits high-fidelity AI generation artifacts, specifically the 'plastic' texture of the tree bark, the physically impossible geometry of the snapped tree trunk, and the uniform, painterly distribution of debris on the ground.
images.explanation: The image is a synthetic AI generation (V1) depicting the described tornado damage, but it is not a genuine photograph.

images.scene_description: A screenshot of a weather application dashboard showing a map of the United States with red 'Tornado Emergency' polygons over Missouri, Illinois, New Jersey, and Hawaii.
images.ocr_text: NEXRAD EMERGENCY | 4 Active Tornado Emergencies | Multiple areas at extreme risk. Take immediate shelter. | 9:38 PM CDT May 25, 2024 | Tornado Emergency St. Louis, MO | Tornado Emergency Southern Illinois | Tornado Emergency Central New Jersey | Tornado Emergency Hawaii
images.v2_evidence: The image displays a date of 'May 25, 2024' which contradicts the post's claim of a 'March 11' event, and includes a 'Tornado Emergency' in Hawaii which is meteorologically implausible and inconsistent with the evidence describing flood monitoring.
images.c_evidence: The image timestamp (May 2024) and specific alert locations (Hawaii Tornado Emergency) mismatch the post's context (March 11) and the evidence's description of Hawaii flood monitoring.
images.explanation: The image is a real screenshot but from a different date and event than the post claims, constituting a context mismatch.

images.scene_description: A screenshot of the 'DisasterAWARE Pro' software interface showing a map of Hawaii with a 'Cumulative Rainfall Forecast' layer active.
images.ocr_text: DisasterAWARE Pro | Layers | Legend | Cumulative Rainfall Forecast (3 Day) | 0 - 0.5 inches | ... | > 30 inches
images.explanation: Clean image matching the post claim and evidence.

images.scene_description: An infographic titled 'TORNADO TERMINOLOGY' with three columns: Watch (yellow), Warning (red), and Emergency (purple), featuring stylized graphics and text definitions.
images.ocr_text: MARCH 11 ACTIVE ALERT STATUS | TORNADO TERMINOLOGY | JanDils ATTORNEYS AT LAW, LC | STORM TEAM | WATCH BE PREPARED! | WARNING TAKE ACTION! | EMERGENCY TAKE ACTION!
images.v1_evidence: The image is a stylized marketing poster with synthetic graphics (perfectly rendered house, radar graphic) and a layout typical of AI-generated or commercial design assets rather than a candid photo or official government chart.
images.v2_evidence: The image contains heavy editorial overlays, including a header banner 'MARCH 11 ACTIVE ALERT STATUS' and a law firm logo 'JanDils', which are graphic design elements added to the canvas.
images.explanation: The image is a synthetic marketing poster (V1) with editorial overlays (V2) used to illustrate the terminology.

images.scene_description: A photo of a collapsed metal building structure with twisted beams and sheet metal debris, next to a white trailer and a red pickup truck.
images.explanation: Clean image matching the post claim and evidence.

images.scene_description: A screenshot of the 'DisasterAWARE Pro' software interface showing a map of Hawaii with 'DFIRM Flood Zones' highlighted in red and pink.
images.ocr_text: DisasterAWARE Pro | Layers | Legend | DFIRM Flood Zones (Hawaii) | 0.2% annual chance (500yr) floodplain | ... | 1% annual chance (100yr) coastal floodplain/storm wave hazard (detailed)
images.explanation: Clean image matching the post claim and evidence.

image_analysis.global_notes: The post uses a mix of genuine screenshots (images 3, 5, 6), a synthetic marketing poster (image 4), a synthetic AI photo (image 1), and a screenshot from a different date/event (image 2).

judge.level3_rationale: The post mixes real weather events with fabricated survey data and misleading visuals, including an AI-generated tornado photo and a weather map screenshot from a different year, resulting in a significantly distorted account of the March 11 weather.
judge.reasoning_trace: The post reports real events (IL tornadoes, HI Kona Low) but fabricates specific details (wind speeds, Indiana emergency) and uses deceptive visuals (AI image, wrong-date screenshot). This combination of T1, V1, and C2 labels pushes the verdict to Mostly False.
judge.core_claim: On March 11, 2026, a widespread severe weather event affected the US, including confirmed EF-1 tornadoes in Illinois, a Kona Low in Hawaii, and tornado emergencies in Indiana and other regions.
key_subclaims: An EF-1 tornado struck Madison County, IL (Moro/Midway) on March 11, 2026, with specific survey stats (110 mph, 1.6 mile path).
An EF-1 tornado struck St. Clair County, IL (Marissa/Lenzburg) on March 11, 2026, with specific survey stats (94 mph).
A tornado emergency was issued in northern Indiana (Knox) overnight into March 11.
The Pacific Disaster Center warned of a Kona Low in Hawaii bringing flood/tornado risks.
The American Red Cross in NJ urged preparedness for severe storms.
Image 1 shows the tornado damage in Illinois.
Image 2 shows the national weather map for the March 11 event.
Image 4 is an infographic explaining tornado terminology relevant to the March 11 alerts.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: An EF-1 tornado struck Madison County, IL (Moro/Midway) on March 11, 2026, with specific survey stats (110 mph, 1.6 mile path).
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms an EF-1 in Moro on March 11, 2026, but does not corroborate the specific wind speed (110 mph) or path dimensions (1.6 miles, 300 yards), which appear fabricated.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: An EF-1 tornado struck St. Clair County, IL (Marissa/Lenzburg) on March 11, 2026, with specific survey stats (94 mph).
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms damage and EF-1 classification in St. Clair County, but does not corroborate the specific 94 mph wind speed.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: A tornado emergency was issued in northern Indiana (Knox) overnight into March 11.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence mentions tornado emergencies in Illinois, Missouri, and NJ, but no evidence supports a tornado emergency in Indiana/Knox. The text likely conflates this with a different event or fabricates it.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The Pacific Disaster Center warned of a Kona Low in Hawaii bringing flood/tornado risks.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence explicitly confirms PDC warnings for a Kona Low with flood and tornado risks.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The American Red Cross in NJ urged preparedness for severe storms.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms Red Cross NJ issued preparedness advisories.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Image 1 shows the tornado damage in Illinois.
subclaim_findings.note: Image 1 appears to be AI-generated (V1) with uncanny features, not a real photo of the specific event.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Image 2 shows the national weather map for the March 11 event.
subclaim_findings.note: Image 2 displays the date 'May 25, 2024', which contradicts the post's claim of March 11, 2026.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Image 4 is an infographic explaining tornado terminology relevant to the March 11 alerts.
subclaim_findings.note: Image 4 is a marketing infographic from a law firm, not an official weather map, but it aligns with the text's explanation of terminology.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post contains a mix of real events (IL tornadoes, HI Kona Low) and fabricated details (specific wind speeds, Indiana tornado emergency). Crucially, it uses AI-generated imagery (Image 1) and out-of-context screenshots (Image 2 with wrong date, Image 4 as marketing material) to support its claims. The presence of T1 (fabricated stats/event), V1 (AI image), and C2 (wrong date in screenshot) creates a high distortion count (5 labels). The core narrative is misleading due to the fabricated specifics and deceptive visuals, fitting the 'Mostly False' criteria (T1 + V1 + companions).

image_findings.explanation: The image exhibits high-fidelity AI generation artifacts (plastic textures, unnatural debris physics) and is presented as a real photo of the event.
The screenshot shows the date 'May 25, 2024', which is a clear temporal mismatch with the post's 'March 11, 2026' context.
Screenshot of DisasterAWARE Pro showing Hawaii rainfall, consistent with the text.
The image is a stylized marketing infographic/poster from a law firm, not a real weather map or official alert, presented as visual evidence.
Photo of structural damage consistent with the text description.
Screenshot of DisasterAWARE Pro showing flood zones, consistent with the text.
